speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This fourth interview with Clara Payne Grove took place at her home in Moscow on December 16, 1975. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I do know that I had a pickup at my door. Well, I suppose at least 20 years ago, and I think it's more than that. And that's the first I knew about it. And then I never thought of it again until they were looking for a person for the position that I have, a representative. They used to be called the center, and then they got high -toned little horn and began to call us representatives. I never knew why they said center because you're no more the center of anything than anybody else than it is. But that's what I was for 11 years until they changed it. It's really an international organization. They have it in various other countries. I don't know just how many. And I'm proud of Moscow. I don't know whether they tell every representative that or not, but maybe they don't. But they say that Moscow turns in more goods than any place in the area. And the area goes from Moses Lake to Missoula, from Walla Walla to the boundary. So it's a pretty good -sized area. But I did a lot of work on it before I got it worked up. I would stand on the street and hand out cards. I'd stop people and tell them about it. And every week I would call 10 on the phone and advertise it. And now I'm not advertising because we're getting all the goods that the big truck will take away. Well, there's no use of us getting more than that will take away. So I'm not advertising now. We do have this slump right now before Christmas. But people don't want to be called on an advertisement right now. They're too busy. So they get a rest from me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wanted to ask you about some of the attitudes in the early days that you remember, the way people felt about things. Because it's pretty clear to me that things have changed quite a bit in the general thinking of people these days. And one thing I wanted to ask you about was the role of women. Because I have this idea that in the early days it was more along the lines of the husband ruled and the wife obeyed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now is that an accurate idea of what it was like then?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, it used to be that there was a very distinct line between the sexes. And while the line is still there, everybody is, well not everybody, but most of the people are doing their best to do anything they can to obscure the line, to erase it. Well, they can't erase it. But they do have it pretty well obscured. But it used to be that a woman's place was defined when it isn't now. Her place is any yahoo thing that she wants to do or work at or play with or anything, that's accepted. And those ridiculous pants suits are more than accepted. They're fairly worshipped.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And at the time I was young, you never heard of such a thing as a woman wearing trousers. In a great emergency when she was compelled to do it, she would put on her husband's, well I was going to say Levi's, I'll tell you how I got reproved for saying that. But she would put on her husband's overalls over her dress for whatever she had to do while cleaning the barn or so on. But she didn't wear it after she went back to the house. It came off when she went in. And there was no such thing as a pantsuit, which, well, I have my own idea of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, if you could describe how a woman's role was defined, how her life was defined in those days, how would it be?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, we did the housework. And I look at it from a farm viewpoint because I was born and raised on a farm. And I'm a farmer yet. But if they were on a farm, the woman took care of the chickens almost entirely if it seldom that a man took care of the chickens. That was woman's work. And more especially in the people of German extraction, the women would carry the swillpail to the hogs. Maybe you don't know what a swillpail is. Well anyway, every kitchen had a swillpail. And that's the name it went by. And into it went, well, we would call it garbage, but into that went the liquid leavings, like a glass of milk that had been partly drunk, or the coffee that was left in the cup or the coffee pot. All the liquids. And if water was scarce, the dishwater always went into the swillpail. And sometimes even if water was not scarce. It was quite common to empty the dishpan when you were through with the dishes into the swillpail. And in a great many homes, a woman carried that and put it in the trough for the hogs.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And she did quite a lot of the garden work. In some cases, in many cases, she did practically all of the garden work. And every farm had a garden, of course.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And as now, it was quite common that she let the family devotions.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And she, in many families, ruled the children more perhaps than is common now. Although, I don't know, perhaps the fathers still don't pay much attention to the children. Leave them to the wife in the winds to grow up as best they can.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And she belonged to the circle of her church. And there were years that it went by the name of the Lady's Aid.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And they didn't divide it up into bunches. It was all the women of the church.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And she taught school, mostly. And there were a few piano teachers.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And in general, they were the head of the children before the children were mature. And after they were mature, they got acquainted with their father more.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And as nearly as I can tell, women were more content to just be a housewife than they are now.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I think they had a real contentment in their role. And they really looked to the men more for opinion and counseling and that sort of thing.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Although, there has always been, this was one that I read and it was good, a woman was entertaining a friend and the hostess' husband came stamping in and told the two women what he thought of the president and the congress and the weather and one thing and another. Until he had finished what he had to say, he said, I am the head of this household and my wife knows it. And he stamped out and when the sound of his footsteps had died away, his wife said, Yes, he's the head of this house and I know it, but I'm the neck that turns the head and he doesn't know it. Well, that sort of defined a woman's position at that time. The husband was the head, but quite a lot of the women were the neck.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Does that sort of define it?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That certainly goes a long ways towards defining it. It's a big question because it involves the whole society. You think that rural, that women in farms and rural areas had a different, much different,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: a much different kind of role than women in towns? Of course, I realize they were on a farm, so they had to do farm tasks, but do you think there was a very large difference between them?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I think there was quite a difference, mostly. There would be exceptions, many exceptions perhaps, but I think mostly. You knew which was the farm woman and which was the town woman. And I think their, well, I think their lives were so different.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: The automobile coming in had a big effect on that matter. If a woman had to go and harness the farm team and hitch them to the lumber wagon, a buggy if they had one, but everybody didn't, but she had to harness the team and hitch them to a vehicle and drive at a rate of, oh, six or eight miles an hour. She wasn't going to do a lot of going. In fact, she wasn't going to do much of any going except on Saturday when father and dad used to be father or papa or pa, but there was no dad.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: But either he would go to town or she would go to town or the whole family would go. And there was this difference. When a mother went anywhere, of course she took the children with her.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: What else could she do?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: There was no such thing as a babysitter and nobody felt the need of it. And it is true. I don't think the children at that time were so much better, really, although we didn't have this prime wave or anything similar to it in any way. But I don't mean to say that they were better, but they were different. That is to say, if the mother took three or four young children with her, she would sit them each on a chair at an age when their feet would stick straight out before them, and they would sit there and they might possibly exchange words with each other once in a while, but they didn't interrupt conversations and they were not noisy. And they didn't ask for anything to play with and they sat there. That was it. And they might sit there for a couple of hours without ever getting down or being among those present at all. So that people at that time were not like the people at this time.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: The adults had to be different or they could not have produced that difference in the children.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: So that...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a demanding of...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: an asserting of authority and demanding that the kids respect the authority of the parents and the rules and be obedient, just unquestioning?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I think that the difference in the feminine and masculine authority over the children was just about the same as it is now. That is to say, I wonder if I can express this just like I wanted. The mother has a field of authority in which the children recognize it and are obedient. And the father has more of an overreach. The mother does not reach into the father's authority, but the father's authority does overreach the household. It's like this. A friend of mine between here and Troy had taken me out to visit her one day and she was going to bring me home. And the grandchildren had come from Troy to Grandma's for a visit. And she was going to bring the children in the car with her when she brought me home. Well, the children got into the car and they started a regular rumpus royal. And Grandma told them they would have to be quiet before she could start the car. And Grandma asked them to be quiet. And Grandma begged them to be quiet. And she urged them to be quiet. And the riot went right on. And Dad stepped up to the car and he just simply looked in at them for a little bit until they got quiet, which they did when Dad looked in there. And he simply said quietly, no fighting in a car, and walked away. Those children didn't put on another bit of rumpus the whole way to town. And that is the difference between masculine and feminine authority over children. I don't know, well yes, I do know why it is. The feminine and the masculine were created in that mold.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And when you come right down to it, it isn't a bad mold.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It means that the father, that the children have a certain amount of fear of their father, which they really don't have of their mother.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Am I correct in saying that?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: They don't feel toward the mother. I don't mean toward the mother, I mean the mother's authority. They don't feel toward it as they do toward father's authority. It can be that there is a mother who is in the habit of even whipping her children to get obedience. And she doesn't get it as father gets it. And perhaps he is always just simply quiet and mild with the children. But the male and the female were created as they were created. And we can't get around it. We can try, but we don't succeed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For the mother on a farm, how would you consider her responsibility as compared to the father's?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean to make decisions and that kind of a thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Her power to make decisions as compared to the father's, did she have to consult with him on any matter of importance?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, depending on what it was. There were areas of strictly feminine work like cooking and so on that she just ad -libbed as she pleased. And she was supposed to. But if it came to any business matter, then the father's word was, unless she could persuade him. Which some others are pretty good at doing sometimes. But the father must be consulted on all business. And if there was something that a child kept doing that was upsetting to the household, and wouldn't stop it for the mother, she relayed it to the father and let him deal with it. So that they recognized the father's authority. In a way they didn't the mother's. And yet there were many things that the mother's authority was first. Intimate things about, especially with little children.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Although it was and still is a fact. That take a young baby that is very fretful and is crying and fussing. And the mother can't quiet it. If the father takes it, frequently the child quiets at once. Well, for one thing, some women say they're as strong as a man, they're not. It doesn't come out that way in a test. And the child doesn't feel any give as though it might slip out from the mother's arm. With the father it doesn't feel that give, it feels the muscle that's under it. And that of itself is quieting. And as a usual thing, the father is longer armed and broader shouldered and makes a more comfortable resting place for the child. And the mother is more equipped, more the size that it needs. So that both father and mother have a place and neither one is greater than that of the other. It's different but not greater.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you express the feeling that the women were more contented in the role of homemaker then than they are now, I find that interesting because it seems as though women had less authority than they have now. They had less, it seems as though. If the man made the decision, I think in today's modern marriage, decisions are looked at as being a matter of mutual consent, perhaps more so than it used to be.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, that is very personal to each and all. It isn't anything a person can generalize. There are women at the present time who do have the old time sense. And there are men who do have the old time sense. But they are not so common. And in my opinion, on the radio, I heard a scientist explain that the chemical effect of the chlorine that was ordered into all drinking water was ordered there by the communists because its chemical effect is to reduce responsibility.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And to me, the irresponsibility of the people at the present time is a shocking thing.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: So I think perhaps that scientist might have been right that chlorine reduces responsibility.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the amount of responsibility that women had in those days was fair, was just? Or do you think that it's been better to widen the rights and opportunities for women to go outside of the home and make their way in any way they can?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, I think that was about the same from the time that it made until today. I doubt if it has varied greatly. While it's true that we have this woman's leave and other foolishness now, but still, down at the foundation of the matter, I think the ratio is perhaps somewhat the same.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: People are people. They're different in different eras. Or, to put it more correctly, they behave differently in different eras. For instance, when I was a child, if I had addressed you as Sam, I would have been in high disgrace. Adults were Mr. and Mrs. and among the adults, they were Mr. and Mrs. unless they were extremely intimate friends or related. We had none of this first name business. It just wasn't done.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that mean that friendships themselves were different in those days?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, the behavior was different. And, in my opinion, the lack of formality that we have now was promoted to promote the sex situation that prevails at the present time. And, I think everybody admits that it does prevail. I know that the formality of our address, the fact that no such thing as male and female going down the street holding hands or with their arms around each other, there was no such thing seen or heard of. It just wasn't known. You couldn't do that. And, I think that formality is a check on loose sexuality.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This makes me think of a question that I had from when we talked before about divorce and about why divorce was considered so wrong in those days. Nowadays, of course, it's common as cake. It's not unusual at all.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, we have the scripture for it that there is only one reason for divorce. Just one. No more. And, the people were closer to following the scriptural mandates than they are now. That would be one reason.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And, another reason. Men and women were more content at that time than they are now. They were not up in the air all the time. They either had or didn't have, well, whichever it was, if they were content with it. I'm old time enough that in a prayer of thanksgiving, I always say that I have everything. Well, some people say I don't have nothing. That my house isn't fit to live in. That it isn't right at all. The furniture isn't right. Nothing's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If I say the truth of the matter, that I am content with it, on what grounds are they going to be able to say that I don't have everything?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: If I felt something was lacking, I couldn't be content. But I say that I have everything, and I do. That is to say, I'm content with what I have. And, when I was a child, people were not rife with nerves like they are now. They didn't have nerves as they do now. They weren't always busy with something or other. Occasionally, there would be someone who kept much busier than anybody else did, and it was noticeable. It was remarkable.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the matter with her, anyway? Nerves, you're thinking of someone that couldn't sit still, for example?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: They could sit still. They could sit quietly down reading the newspaper. They didn't have to have a Wild West. And the newspapers were clean. And there wasn't so much reporting of what crime and so on that there was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it, when you say a person who was nervous as standing out, what would a nervous person be like? Someone who couldn't sit still?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Someone who was wild with nerves was noticeable as one. Not as the mass, but as one. And they were very noticeable. There was a contentment prevailing. And I went through hard times.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And people took it, well, took it hard. But they didn't go wild about it to other people. If they had any wildness, they kept it to themselves.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I remember a mother always feeding the men who came to the door, asking for a handout.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And one time a neighbor woman told me, yes, someday her mother's going to get killed by one of those men. Well, I ran home quickly and told her. And she said, no, no man is as dangerous as a hungry man. So I just feed him. Well, that's logic. And a man who is hungry is more important than one who isn't. Mother just fed them over and over and over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were young and growing up, a man or a woman who was divorced, was a person divorced regarded as someone not trustworthy?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: They were not quite accepted like other people were. They were just a little on the outer fringe. And divorces were so rare.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: As much as, oh, something like 60 years ago, a bunch of us were together one day and one of them spoke about a recent divorce.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And when the others had thrashed it over, this one woman said, well, I always say if I can't get along with the husband I have, how do I know I'll get along with any other one?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, that was the opinion of, she had expressed the opinion of the world. That if you can't do with one partner, maybe you wouldn't do with any other. And there was very little divorce.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And I don't remember just when it was, but I do remember that I began to notice when a man would divorce his wife. Way back when I was a child and so on, a man wouldn't think of getting a divorce. He might threaten his wife into her getting a divorce for his benefit, but he didn't ask for a divorce any more than he'd said, well, go on and open the doors and put my coat on me and do the things you should. And that's the way it was. It just wasn't done. But I noticed in the paper now and then that a man had divorced his wife.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The age of marriage for young people, was it much different when you were growing up than now?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well,

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I think I could do very well say there was no such thing as double teen marriage. It's true that a girl might marry at 18 or 19, which was considered young. And way back in my grandmother's time, in the 18s, 20s, 30s, and 40s, it was considered that a girl should be married. Before she was 20, definitely. She must be married in her teens.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: But I don't remember any marriage way back in which both were teenage. A girl might marry a man who could vote. That was a recognized age of some importance. And it was 21, which should be 25 at the present time, instead of 18.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did young people learn responsibilities at a younger age?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: As to what they learned, they learned responsibility about living. Whereas now, they learn science and all that kind of thing. There was that sharp difference.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: The young people might go through high school, but the necessity for taking anything beyond high school, it wasn't considered there was any necessity for it. Of course, some did go to college, but not by hordes. It was a few who went to college.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: We had a college in the town in which I lived for some years in my youth.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: It was known as Redfield College, which it wasn't. But it was in Redfield, South Dakota. First it was in Redfield, Dakota Territory, before the state was divided. It was a congregational school maintained by the congregational church in general. Not of the town, but the general conference.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You describe to me the story of the young.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: The young people who went to college had men and women well up in their 20s and some of them in their 30s who were learning geography and math and all that kind of thing because they hadn't been to high school. We did have one little boy there. He was young enough that he was still wearing knee trousers. That was a mark of whether you were considered adult, whether you wore knee trousers or could have long ones. This little fellow was still wearing the long black stockings and knee trousers. His mother had died. His father was a pastor of a church in some town near. He had this one boy on his hands when his wife died. He didn't know how to care for the boy and go on with his pastorate work. I think at the present time, any boy like that would have been considered that he could forage for himself. He didn't need any looking after or anything. But anyway, whether he'd interfered with his pastorate or whether he thought it was too lonesome for the boy or whatever, he sent him to college. And there he was with this group of men. I used to think just how lonesome he must feel in the dorm, at the table, in class, everywhere, all with all grown people.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: But he was there the rest of the school year. I don't know what became of him then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the life and town life in the early days when you were growing up?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, for one thing, the farm women in general did not keep up with the fashions like the town women did. Of course, farming at that time was not a very lucrative proposition. The crops were poor and the prices were low. So that in some cases it was impossible. In many cases. But in general, the farm women did not keep up with the fashions like the town women did.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And since it is more difficult to attend school if you live out of town than if you live in town where the school is. And the school that is near you goes only through the eighth grade. There was a school on every quarter section. But it went only through the eighth grade. And in general, they did not have as much book education as the town women did. Although, so far as newspapers were concerned, I think they were receiving more attention from farm women than they were from town women. The farm women did not go to the evening meetings, whatever it was, which would be held in town. But she would read the paper and she knew what the news was. And she knew what politics was doing. Although, I don't know that women ever talked politics. I never heard them talking it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you think she knew what was going on in any case?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I think she knew pretty well whatever was in the newspaper.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that people, the farm people in the country had a certain amount of distrust of the townspeople and their way of living?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, the cooking was far different in town than in the country.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: In the country, if you were mashing the potatoes, you put in a big lump of butter and half a cup of cream. In town, you put in a quarter of a cup of skim milk. There was that difference in cooking. And in town, now and then, they could buy a steak. They could buy a steak for a quarter.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: My father used to go downtown and buy a quarter's worth of steak. And the family was fed steak.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And people on the farms mostly did their own butchery. So that they had hams and bacon and steak and roast and everything. And that made a big difference in how the cooking went on.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And the very fact that the woman helped with saving the new calves, like taking it into the kitchen if it was a cold weather calf until it got on its feet and full of milk. And she took care of the chickens.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And now and then, she took the horses to water. And you know, handling livestock puts something into the system that isn't there otherwise. It makes a little difference.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Perhaps I should tell you, it's a story of the early, early days in Moscow. Very early days. I heard it when I first came here. It happened just right down across here on the creek. There was one house the other side of the creek. The man who lived in it was in the habit of, after he was through his day's work, coming into town and getting pretty well hopped up with liquor. And to go home across the creek to where he lived, there was just a log to cross the creek on. So one time he had gotten pretty well filled up with liquor. And he started home. And he got across the log without falling in. It was currently told that he always fell into the creek. It would be, of course. But this time he didn't fall in and he went to the door of this one house and knocked.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And a woman opened the door and he said, Lady, would you please tell me where, he mentioned his name, lives?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And she said, you do, you old fool.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Don't you know your own house?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: He said, no, Lady, I don't live here. I didn't fall in the creek.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: That was a favorite story here in the early days.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And in those early days when I came, that wasn't early. The old timers would tell me, you've hardly arrived yet when I've been here 20 or 30 years. But Hayes Street was practically that edge of the town. And just on the other side of Hayes Street, there was a house in, oh, a few acres of ground that were cultivated. Right there facing on Hayes.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And, uh, Archer Avenue was, the whole thing was cleared out in the country. And the settlement that's just south of the campus was the King Dairy.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And just far south of the creek, there was a house on the hill above. And one or two down closer to the creek.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you grew up in South Dakota, you must have seen much more the tail end of that, of the real pioneering. Because I imagine there was still a lot of the homesteaders in the country where you grew up.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I've seen a lot of pioneering. I was raised in it in South Dakota. And not South Dakota, Dakota Territory. I was about four when we went on to Father's Homestead there. And that was Pioneer. No fooling about that. And we had a large claim house. It was, well it was larger than these two rooms a little, I'd say. But, uh, it wasn't as large as the whole house, I would say. Well, that was quite a large house. So they had school in our house. And my aunt, Ellen Conner, taught the school. And in those times, a rule was a rule. And that was it. And it was a rule that a child did not go to school until he was six. Well, I was five and my brother was two years younger. And then we had four older than we were. Two girls and two boys. Well, that four were in school. But Freddie and I were not old enough for school. And with Mother, who went on with her baking or sewing or whatever, we had one end of the school room. And the school was in the other end. And at one time, a terrible storm. It might have been a blizzard. I don't remember it. But, anyway, as it struck and before it immediately got thick, everybody was started home. And Ellen, who was teaching, and her youngest brother was in school. And her oldest brother's older children were in school. That was the school. And they were all started home. It all got home.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And I saw pioneer life there. And I saw pioneer life in North Dakota in the end of the 1800s. And there, the people were very interesting. There was the Slav Russian, and the German Russian, and the Scandinavians. And here and there, there were some Americans. But they were not too common. And one of the Slav Russians had built his house, I think as they did in Russia, of Sade. And as they did in all of the new countries built of Sade. Because there wouldn't be anything else. And the Sade house, properly put up, is a real house. The blizzards don't go through it, and the heat of the sun in summer don't go through it. But they had built their house of Sade, and they had built the windows right in with the Sade. They didn't open it all. And the only way into the house was through the door that opened into the barn. You went through the barn, down the alley between the two rows of stock. Then you got into the house where the windows could not be opened. And that was the only door. Well, my brother was in a harvest crew that traded work at this place. At that time, for harvest, or anything that required more men, they traded work. They didn't hire. And he was in the crew that was trading work. And he said it was just almost over more than he could live through to sit at the table while they had dinner. But that's the way they lived.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: One of the girls was in my school. Her hair was black, but actually her hair was grey with the lights that were in it. And she just endured them. The Slav Russian is of a very low walk -down order. They have never had any opportunity at all. And they are very low people. But of the German Russians, I had in one school a little girl named Amanda Marknakowski. Her father had died. Her mother was Russian, so she spoke Russian. And her stepfather was German, so she spoke German. And she spoke both of the languages. She was eight years old. She spoke both of the languages fluently. And spoke English without any accent of either of her other languages. So there she was at eight years, competent with three languages. She had big grey eyes. And when she stood to recite, she looked straight into my eyes and never turned her gaze once. And whatever I said, she could repeat verbatim any time afterwards. She was a very good child. She took it all. I never had such a remarkable child as she was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I didn't know that she taught school.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Oh, I taught for seven years before I married. I taught in South Dakota, North Dakota, and Montana. And in Montana, it was new there when I went there. Not the really new. But they told me a good many of the early day tales. And they would tell me about Brother Van. Brother Van was the Reverend William Van Orsdel.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Van Orsdel?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Van Orsdel. D -O -R -S -E -L -L. Van Orsdel. He was the first Methodist missionary ever to go to Montana Territory. Well, it was a state at the time I went there, but it had been a territory when he went there.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And he got with the people to the extent that he had no name but Brother Van to such an extent that in his final illness, he said, Now on my stone put nothing but two dates and the words Brother Van. So his stone is marked with two dates and Brother Van. And on Rock Creek, that was settled a little more than Trout Creek where I lived. And they were a slightly different set of people. And he went there preaching at certain intervals, stated intervals. Everybody would turn out for the preaching. And they wanted him to come and have Thanksgiving dinner. In that neighborhood, the people all got together every Thanksgiving day and had a general dinner. And they wanted him to join them. And he said, Well, I'll tell you what I'll do. He said, If you'll build a church here like you ought to, I'll have Thanksgiving dinner with you every year as long as I live. Which he did as long as he was able to travel that far. But he got a horse and he went horseback just as other people did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he have to ride a circuit? Did he have a wide area to cover?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He rode a circuit and he rode a horse.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And he got in with the Indians. There wasn't anybody he couldn't make friends with. And the Indians took him on a buffalo hunt. And they waited very politely for him to shoot the first buffalo, which he did. And then they, with their arrows, got their buffalo. But he was riding on a part of his circuit one night. It was dark. Rain was pouring. And he thought, Well, nobody is going to go to that schoolhouse a night like this. If I see a light anywhere, I'm going to stop and ask to spend the night. Well, he saw a light.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: He saw a light, so he went to the door. A girl with a shotgun in her hands came to the door. He introduced himself and asked if he could spend the night. She said, No, you can't. She said, We have only one spare bed and we're keeping that for Brother Van. And we can't keep you. And my father and mother are at the schoolhouse now waiting for Brother Van. He got back in the saddle and went on in a hurry. And they took him home with them at the close of the meeting. And this girl he became engaged to. And just days before they were to be married, without any explanation that anybody could think of, she died. And he never paid the slightest attention to any woman, especially, you know, after that. And just lived by himself and went on with his work. And at one time, someone hunted up Brother Van in a hurry and they told him that the Indians had taken a certain man captive. And that they were camped about two miles from Grigory Flat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And would he be able to do anything about getting the man?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, he got into his saddle and went out there in a hurry. When he got to Grigory Flat, he dropped the reins so his horse would stay there. And started this two miles back to where the Indians would be camped. And when he got back where the dogs started barking, he knew he'd been heard. That the dogs knew he was coming and therefore the people knew that something was doing. He started singing. He loved to sing. He had a sort of a mezzo tenor voice. Not a clear high tenor, but a very good tenor voice. And he did love to sing. And he started singing when the dogs started barking. Well, when he got to the camp, the Indians were sitting in a circle around their campfire. And he just simply went and sat down among them. And sat there until the chief began after a while to talk to him. Well, the chief passed the peace pipe. And he took his pull on the peace pipe and passed it on. And after a while, the chief spoke to him. Well, then he said why he had come to see this man. And he let the Indian lead the talking. And he'd talk a little now and then. And he seemed to know just the thing to say. So after a while, the chief told someone to go and cut the man's bindings loose. So the man got on his feet and the chief told Brother Van he could go now and take the man with him. So they left. Well, that was Brother Van. And there is the story. I didn't hear it direct that I've heard it. But this I heard so direct that there was no question on the story at all. But it was said that Brother Van always wore his pulpit clothing. So they would recognize him as a divine. And he was wearing his pulpit clothing. And a posse came along hunting for a horse thief. And one of the posse said, that's the man. So they seized Brother Van. And he said, no, he wasn't the thief. Well, we'll hang you anyway and then we'll ask you questions when we get you hung. Well, he said he wasn't Brother Van. He wasn't the thief. He was Brother Van. And one of the men said, well, I know how we can tell whether he's Brother Van or not. I've heard him sing. I can't think what the story was of him. But I've heard him sing that. Let him sing it now and I'll know whether that's Brother Van or not. So Brother Van started on the hymn. And after a little this man said, that is Brother Van. There is no question about it. That is Brother Van. And this time he wasn't wearing his pulpit suit for some reason. But after that he never was out without his pulpit suit on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What part of Montana was this? Where in Montana?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: It was the other side of Great Falls. Between Great Falls and Lewiston and Harlow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like being a teacher for you in those years that you taught in the little school houses?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I imagine that conditions must have been fairly rugged then.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, they were rugged, but we were rugged too. And you can believe it or not, my very first school I had $25 a month. My own. $25 every month. And I had about a dozen pupils I think. There might have been a bit more than that, but not much if there was any.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And at that time they handed you a contract and a broom and a fire shovel and you were all ready to teach. So, they told me I could board with Mrs. Meade. So I went over to Mrs. Meade's and they told me it was a little over a mile. About a mile. Well, to me a mile was nothing. I could walk it in 15 minutes without hurrying. And I went over and she said yes, she would keep me for $10 a month. Well, that wasn't my first school, that was my second. But I said, well I paid only $8 for my other school. She said yes, but I have to have $10 and I'll do your washing too. Well, that was something. So for $10 I had my board and lodging and washing. And then I had 15 left.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: We had reading, writing, and arithmetic. That's what we had. And if they were old enough they had a geography book. And you were just somebody when you got to a place where you could have a geography book. But you had a reader and you had a speller.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And could they spell?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: They learned the letters that went into each word and they knew how to say them. I tapped my bell and the next class would come up and stand in front of me. I was sitting on my rostrum about a foot or a little higher, maybe 18 inches high. I was sitting at my desk up there and they stood before me. They recited for 15 minutes and I tapped my bell and they went and sat down and went on with their work.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And they had good times together. There was one school I taught in which these little Slav Russian boys came in and they were crying. And they said the girls won't let me play. And I said, I knew why.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, well why don't they let you play?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: They said our hands are too dirty. I said, well when you go home you wash your hands with soap until they're just real clean all over. And the girls will let you play tomorrow. Well they did. They had orders. But oh their hands. You can't imagine that a human being could be as dirty as those boys were. And they did. They washed their hands. They didn't go any farther.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: But I felt sorry for the poor little youngsters.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find that there were wide differences in ability among the children that you taught? Did you find that among the children that you taught there were wide differences in ability?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Yes, there was. Yes, the second school that I spoke of there was a boy who, well I wouldn't say he wasn't bright. He was just an ordinary youngster. He was a very good teacher but he couldn't take books as fast as the rest of them did. And I was too inexperienced to recognize the problem for what it was and work with it as could have been done. But he was a nice little boy and about, oh about living. You didn't see any difference in him than any of the rest of them. But I did have, in another school I had a girl that came to school just a little while. She was the oldest in the family and she was 10 or 11 at this time. And because she had never been in school enough to learn to use a book, it was very difficult for her and she just came just a little while. They were Russian but not Slav. And I did have some that were really bright. And I had one boy that stammered quite badly. And I wrote a note to his mother and asked her if there was something that I could do to help him get over it. And she sent back a note saying, no just let him alone, don't say anything about it, just let him alone. Well I'm not sure that was the thing to do. But I did. But he did stammer badly. And they, well that held him backward because he was so self -conscious. But the brother Oder was one of those people that just takes with everybody. He was a wonderful boy.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And then they had, there were two, well they really were adult sisters. They were, I think 15 and 17. They were the two first of the children. And they had, they had six children in school. And they had two more at home at the same time. And I heard afterward that they had one or two more than that. And their farming wasn't panning out at all and they were practically living on nothing. But the mother was a very superior person. Very superior. And she took in sewing with all that family.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And did those youngsters know how to work?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: They'd better learn. And I know I was there one time when she asked the oldest girl to do something or other. And the girl was dipping something into something. And she turned to one of the others and she says, hand me that.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And the mother said, Ollie, when you start something, before you start it, you say to yourself, now what am I going to need while I do that?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And you get it. That's the way she brought her youngsters up. And they were nice children. And the father was such a good soul. Slow, but so good.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Was the lot of this family unusual or was it common that the families there were having a rough time making a go of it?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, six at that time was not considered a large family.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: If you went beyond six, it was getting a little sizable. But six was not a large family.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: What I mean, their lot as far as having a hard time getting along like they were having, was that common for families in the area? Was everybody struggling? Most people struggling?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Nearly all of them.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Nearly all of them were having a struggle. For one thing, it just wasn't a crop climate. But there was a time there in the earliest days that the annual rainfall was 14 inches. Well, that's supposed to be below anything that will raise a crop. And they hadn't gotten on to how to farm as much as they did later. And then later, I heard one year, in a letter, about that the crops were being rained out. I said, well, I'll believe that when I see it. Well, in that same summer or the next, I was on a train from Huron to Redfield. And in building their railroads, they always made a ditch alongside. And it was gumbo land. And gumbo holds the land, holds the rain almost like plastic would. And right beside the track in this ditch, that was about that wide, I think, corn was planted. And water was standing inches deep on that corn, and the corn was yellow and puny. I'd seen crops rained out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What area was this that you're describing where you were teaching? What part of Dakota?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: It was, well, I began in Falk County. Well, all my teaching was in Falk County. That was my certificate was for Falk County. And it's way east of the Missouri River. Aberdeen on the north and Huron on the south. Brookings on, no, Aberdeen on the north. Huron, I was here and was about as much south as it was east, as it was south. But there was Brookings and Redfield was the town for that part right there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, were most of the people on the farm still optimistic of being able to be successful, or was that period of boom already passed?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: In the very early days, there was a stream of prairie schooners, that is covered wagons, going west and a stream of them going east. And one of them was going east had on it a sign that said, going back to wife's folks.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: So that was for some years like that. And then after a while, I think people began to believe that they were going to make a success out of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Thank you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, we're talking about, oh, I think one more thing about this teaching.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What made you decide to become a teacher?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Because my older sister compelled me to. She knew what was proper for me. She had the idea. I wanted to be a nurse. I wanted to take training.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I might even see a man in his bed if I nursed. She wasn't going to have me in any occupation like that. So I had to teach.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And I guess I made a success out of it. I had a letter from the chairman of the board of directors of one school that I might teach his school anytime I wanted to. And nobody complained that I know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it hard for you to teach because it's not what you wanted to do? Or did you just go ahead and do it anyway?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, I liked it. I liked it.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I enjoyed it. I've done quite a lot of kinds of work. Enough that when at 82 I matriculated over here at the university.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In this little space at the bottom that says, what occupations have you followed?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I filled all of that space and went all around all the margins with different jobs I'd worked at. But I can't say that there were any of them that I objected to really. They were alright. It was work. It had its remuneration to some extent at least. I don't think I hated anything I did no matter what it was. And in the teaching, well, there were interesting things that came up and pleasant things and sometimes it would be something funny. I really enjoyed it. I did one quiz. I had asked the question, name some natural curiosity of South Dakota.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: One boy wrote cabbages and apples. He was right. Cabbages and apples were very scarce.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: But it was an odd reply.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But it wasn't what?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: It was an odd reply.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Talking about circuit riding, I was wondering, did you ever attend a revival?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Any other religious revivals?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Yes, I have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were they like then?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, I attended one that impressed me more than any other one. It was in a church. They had a small church at Garnier, Montana.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And the people of the little settlement were there and there were some people who had come some miles to attend the meeting.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: This, I think, was not further ran. No, it was not further ran.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: The building was filled to a measure that it was physically felt. Not only myself, but everybody felt the Holy Spirit filling that building.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Everybody was held spell -bound.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And one man rose to testify. He was just an uneducated sheepherder. And he rose and faced us. He had his cap in his hands. And he rolled his cap over and over and over. And the preacher said, Well, the brother has nothing that he can say to us. The man was totally unable to say one word.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: But he made his testimony.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And,

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: My brother told me of a meeting in a town in Montana. I wish that I could remember the name of that town, but I can't. It's toward Great Falls. Not so far out of Great Falls. But they were having a revival there. And this brother of mine and two uncles went in from where they were camped in their prairie schooner and attended the meeting. And the brother said that when the altar call was made, the people just sat. There was no movement, whatever. They just sat. And the pastor tried talking. He wasn't getting anywhere. And of these two uncles of mine, one of them was very dignified and quiet and backward. And the other one was of the cannonball type. In fact, he was elected to the legislature at the time the working men were trying to put over the 8 -hour law. And to the time he died, he had one of the pens with which the governor signed the bill. And they put him in to work for the bill. And it was largely his work that got it in. Well, he was just that cannonball type. He could just be sitting and he'd just rise up. And everybody would notice him. And he'd start to speak and they'd just sit there with their eyes on him, listening. So when the preacher was having no success with his altar call, Uncle Charlie just simply got up and started talking. And my brother said that in just minutes, two or three minutes, the altar was full of kneeling people.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And he himself, I don't know that he ever claimed to be Christian or whether he didn't. I don't know. But anyway, just the fact that he was talking.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: He's the only one anywhere in the whole connection that I ever knew of being like that. I do have a nephew who makes friends with everybody. He told me one time, it's a fact, he said he could stand on a street corner in any city in the United States and inside of an hour, somebody would pass him standing on the street corner that he knew from reading about him or had met or personally knew. He said he had tried it and there never was a time but in an hour that person would pass. And the memory he had, he could go back to the time he was four or five years old and tell you exactly what had happened, at what place, when. And one time someone, I had a bunch of college men eating here one time and my nephew was here a while. One time one of them mentioned a baseball game some years back, something special about it that they'd been talking baseball and he'd mentioned this. And this nephew of mine spoke up and said yes and the score was, he named the two Thames and the score of each of them several years back. I never saw anything like it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Before we get off the subject of revivals, I wanted to know more about what that revival was like, the one that you were at, the one you mentioned that was so moving. Because we have little record in this county, in this area, of what the revivals were like, what was involved in them. These people were stricken with it, with the spirit, but there was no response to the altar call. No, I don't mean the one with your brother, I mean the one before that. You described being in the hall with the feeling of the Holy Spirit.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: That was in a church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In a church?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Yes, in a church. Well, that's the one I mean.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: That's the one I mean when I say that the people were stricken, but there was no response. Which I couldn't understand. But that's the way it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did they usually go? Were they long, the revivals? Did they last for days or just for a short period of time?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: They might last for two or three or four evenings, or they might be just one evening. Frequently, we're only one evening. More and more frequently. And then there were the camp meetings, and they lasted a week or so. And at the camp meetings, they just had a series of service right down through the day. Made use of all the time they were there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I never went to the camp.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: But it was a session of classes with learning, and of the great speakers of the nation speaking on whatever that speaker's topic was. They were an educational affair. And they were quite largely attended. And that was a paid attendance. You paid for attending.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, to think of what religion, what Christianity was like in those days, was the, for instance, was the Sabbath observed very strictly?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: With many people it was.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: My grandmother observed it so strictly that one day when my sister said to her, Grandmother, are you going to wash tomorrow?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And grandmother said, I tried to reserve the Sabbath. She wouldn't say yes or no. And there was, when I lived near Garnier, there was a Phillips family there. But the mother and the sons went on running the farm after the father died. And one day a neighbor man rode over, and Mrs. Phillips was a great worker. And here was Mrs. Phillips all dressed up in her Sunday best. And he wondered how that was, but she asked the man in, and took him into the parlor. And here sat the Phillips family and their Sunday best sitting around in the room. And he talked a little while, and Mrs. Phillips was just as usual, and everybody else was, only they were just sitting dressed up. And finally he said, Mrs.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Phillips, is there something wrong? Has something happened to you? Is anything the matter? She said, well, why do you ask?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, he said, I was surprised when you folks were, the fields continually, that you were all sitting here dressed up. She said, I always observed the Sabbath that way. He said, yes, but this is Monday.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And she said, well, anyway we observed the Sabbath, go get your work clothes on and go to work.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: She was quite a woman.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And she said, yes, but she was just as usual, and everybody else was just sitting dressed up. And the use of scripture then, I get the idea that scripture and the quotation in citing scripture was much more important than in Christianity today.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that true?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It is true.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: We had the King James version of the scripture, and we didn't need anything else to go with it. We used that, and it was very common. And most of the homes had a great big family Bible. A book about that thick probably, and of something like that size. And it had the King James version of the Bible, and then it had blank leaves for family records and all that. And that was very common. We never knew what became of ours. I had a sister who married a widower, and when my sister died, his children went in and took what they wanted and burned the rest. And we don't know whether they took the Bible or burned it.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And my sister had grandmother's side saddle. It would be worth $5 ,000 right now. Well, whether they took it or burned it, we don't know. But anyway, that's the way things go. You can just say with belief in it that nothing is safe. Anything is likely to happen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The preacher that you were telling me about, the preacher who you were just telling me about before who had all the adventures and was so well known. Van Orsdale.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you see him?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Oh yes, I knew him personally. At one time, he and I were both on the train from Moore to Lewiston. It isn't Lewiston, it's Lewiston. We were both on the train. He was sitting in the back of the coach with a bunch of men, and they were having a good time laughing and joking. And as we all rose to go out when the train got to Lewiston, I waited for the jam to get out of the door. I was in no hurry, like jamming with the rest of them, so I simply stood until a little of the crush would be by.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he came along, and he was a great big tall man, and he stooped down over me and he said, Oh sister, I am so frightened, can you tell me which bus to take? Well, there was one cab, and he said, can you tell me which cab to take?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: There was one horse -drawn cab in the town. And he'd known the place when it was just a village. But that was Brother Van, he always had to have his fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was he like to listen to as a preacher?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: He didn't have sermons. He'd get up to preach a sermon.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And, well, he'd quote some scripture and maybe he'd make a few remarks.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And he'd say something like, I know you boys are having a hard time, but be as good as you can, won't you?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And then he'd start singing. And everybody would join in and they'd sing a hymn, and then he'd talk a little longer. And he'd hope their crops were coming well. He didn't preach a sermon at all.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: But he did put some very pointed remarks in here and there that fitted, and he knew how to fit them. But you couldn't say that he ever preached a sermon. But he did love to sing. And his favorite song was, Beyond the Smiling and the Weeping, I Shall Be Soon. Well, he wasn't very soon. His girlfriend was, sure. But he was getting, I think, pretty well into the 70s, perhaps. I'm not sure just how old he was, but he wasn't a young man, by any means.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These stories that you were telling about him, I take it these are stories that were fairly well known by the congregation, by the people back there?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Yes, he just, just come and talk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The time that he came to the house at night and then went on to the meeting from there, did you ever hear whether there were many people at that meeting?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, I never did.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Her parents were there, anyway. Her parents were there. But I never heard whether there were other people or anything about it.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Because they'd told the story. That was it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you very much talk about going to heaven or being damned when you were young, in the services, in church, being saved?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, distinctly, there was a heaven and a hell. And some preachers harped on the two, and other preachers didn't mention them. In Redfield, at one time, we had an extremely remarkable man. For his sermon that was preached this Sunday, that they,

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: of the teachers meeting, when the certificates were, the examinations were made and the certificates were awarded. The group practically all went to his church Sunday morning that they were there. And knowing that they would be there. He had a collection of small stones that he had picked up here and there.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: There were plenty.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And he compared life to those stones until, I wasn't at church that Sunday, my sister was.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And she said that as they went out, the teachers were all saying, wasn't that the most remarkable sermon you ever heard? Did you ever hear a better sermon?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: He had just captivated his audience with it. And he was very much given to small words that are a common use. He didn't have to use five or six syllables to a word to express himself. And he just talked plain everyday language in a plain everyday way. But he did preach remarkable sermons, and they were real sermons. But I can't remember him ever talking about heaven and hell.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: But he brought it right to the people today.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: He had come out on recommendation of a doctor that he bring his wife to a different climate. But she died in spite of all that could be done for her. And he made himself very unpopular by later on, I don't know, a couple of years or so. He married his wife's niece, who had taken care of his wife in her last time. Well, his wife's niece was no relation to him. And there were some of the people who couldn't see there was anything wrong in him marrying her. But some of them were very bitter about it.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: But he was a remarkable man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say his sermons were remarkable, does that mean that when this idea that he would get it down to the people and make it mean something in their lives?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He could reach them.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Yes, yes, whatever he said reached the people. They understood it. And I think they took it to heart. I know he was very popular up to the time he made his second marriage.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, I'm trying to learn and understand more about the difference between country and town in the early days. And I've had the idea from things that people have said that towns often were not as friendly to country people when they moved into town. That towns were in some ways more closed than the country neighborhoods that were friendlier and more open.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that true, do you think, or is that just a depend too much?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I think perhaps that wasn't true to any great extent. It might have been localized here and there to a marked degree, but in general I wouldn't think there was such a great difference. And there wasn't so much of country people moving into town as there is now.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: They probably just stayed on the farm until they died.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think that the town people were more caught up in the idea of getting ahead and getting a better status in life than country people?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: No, I don't. It's true that they might make more display, but I think the country people were just as ambitious and just as much interested in getting on as any town people could be.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: But a person might have that idea from the fact that back in those early days the farming was so far from being remunerative, as it has been at times since, that the people had no opportunity to get ahead as perhaps someone in the town did. And so they might have thought the town people were more ambitious with the people in the country working about 18 hours a day and hoping and doing everything they could to get ahead. Near Redfield there was one farmer, and why others didn't take a pattern from him I don't know. He planted wheat, oats, barley, spilts, corn.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: He planted all the crops, about so much of it. Well, some of it would fail and some of it didn't every year. There was never a year that he didn't have some plots that gave him a return. And he had horses and mules and cattle and sheep and hogs and chickens and turkeys and ducks and geese. People would sometimes laugh at him about having such a mixture. He'd say, well, I get something. If I don't get it from one, I get it from another. And yet nobody took a pattern from him and he wasn't having a hard time. He was well to do.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: His son was going to college. Well, that meant that he could pay the college expenses and hire someone to take the son's place on the farm. And it didn't worry him any.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: He really made a success of what he did. Well,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: this idea of becoming successful and getting ahead in the early days, if farming wasn't paying well, then it seems like almost everybody that farmed wouldn't be doing too well until things changed quite a bit. And it seems like I wonder why many more people didn't become discouraged or if they felt somehow believed in themselves that they were going to get ahead someday if they stuck with it long enough.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: People at that time were more stalwart than they are now and they didn't become discouraged as much as people do now. They didn't become anything extrovert as much as people do now. They had a trail that they followed. If there was no trail there, they made one. And you just couldn't move them like you can people now. You didn't hear all this moving around and so on. And at that time, practically all of the old people, when they needed care, were cared for by their own. It is true right now that in order to be employed, a great many men must move frequently. And it is true that when he gets into this next town, a one -room apartment or at best a one -bedroom apartment is what he can pay for. Well, you can't take your parents into a place like that, although people could before. I know a place in which a man and his wife, when they were old, were living with their son. And I'm sure they had only a one -room shanty. And they lived there until the man died there. And he died on the Fourth of July. That is, he died on the Third of July. Well, his body had to lie right there until burial. So, they buried him the morning of the Fourth of July. And the people all came to the funeral before they went to celebrate. But there they lived, that old couple and the son in that one -room shanty. And there he died and there he lay until they could bury him. Those things were not so uncommon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, the trouble with the people now, in my opinion, began with the dole, the WWO, or what was it? The WPA?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Yes, the WPA and that kind of thing. That was handed out to the people to break their backbones, and it did. And seeing that that broke their backbones, they've done more until they've broken us all up. But we had no such thing then. Our near ancestors, we didn't go way back to find it. The next generation back of us had probably been absolutely pioneers to a place where there was nothing. Where no human being had ever been. Well, you have to have backbone if you go to that place and come out. And a good many lived there and either got out and pioneered elsewhere or lost the pioneer spirit and stayed on what they had there. But they had a backbone that the people now don't know anything about at all. We're soft.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In those memories of pioneering that you heard and what you knew about, in cases where the conditions were just rugged, the very beginnings like that, do you know of what different kinds of effort that demanded of, let's say, the women, the wives in that kind of situation? Did women do the things that they, under normal conditions, would never have to do? To get by?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: They'd get by in any way that they could.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: It wasn't how do we do it, it was do we arrive?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And they arrived.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: There were few deaths because of pioneering. In fact, I don't think I know of any death that was caused by pioneering. Back in my family, there were three sisters who lived together in a cabin in the woods. And in the spring one day, two of the sisters were out cleaning up the winter fall from the trees and so on, cleaning up around the house outside. And one of the sisters was in the house. It happened that she was the smallest of the three. But she was in the house and the other two cleaning up outside. And one of them looked up from her, reeking outside, and she said, sisters, a bear! And she and the sister that were outside ran in, and the sister that was inside grabbed the door and let them in, and then she slammed the door, and the three of them put their weight on the door. And they had it with the bear's head in and his neck caught in the door. Well, this youngest sister happened to be the most expert, the little one. She wasn't little because she was young, she was just the smallest. But being the smallest, even then, she was the most expert with an axe of any of the sisters. So the two heavier sisters held the door, and of course they kept the axe in the house.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Where would they keep it?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: They had nothing but the house. She grabbed the axe and she chopped the bear's head until the bear fell dead. And then the sister fell down with exhaustion. Well, anyway, they had a bear skin. They could make moccasins or they could make a rug or whatever it was they needed. Although it was in the spring, and the bear was almost meatless from its winter hibernation, but still it would make soup at least. So they were in. And they didn't think anything of that, it was just what came up and they had to do it, and they did it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that extremely rare for a woman or women to pioneer by themselves?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: No, it wasn't rare at all for a woman to be alone a long time. Now whether there was anyone else who lived in this cabin at any time, I never heard. I just heard this much. But for a woman to be alone endlessly while her husband was, well, gone to market a hundred miles away or was hunting or whatever.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Maybe out getting work someplace else.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: There's an incident in our own family. The man, now when I say he was a hunter, I'm saying the same thing as though I had said he was a doctor, he was a blacksmith, he was anything else. A hunter was a man who professionally was a hunter, and he made his living that way and provided skins and meat on a cash basis. Well this man was a hunter, and he would be gone sometimes long times on a hunt. And he was gone a long time. And when he would go home, when he would get nearly home, close to home, he would go cautiously until he would get in sight of the house to see whether he had a house left there or whether the Indians had burned it down. And they might still be around the fire. Well, he was going cautiously the last way through the timber, and he got where he could see. And here was his wife, and the three children, the youngest, a baby in arms, and one of two years or so old and one older than that. And his wife had saddled her horse and led it up to the stump that was the block. And she had the baby in her arms and the two year old behind the saddle and the younger one standing on the block with her, with the baby in her arms. And she was trying to get on with the baby in her arms and get the boy on the block up behind the saddle with the other child. But the horse, with anyone on the block, would not go near it. It would keep circling back away from the block. And she couldn't reach the one there because she couldn't get the horse up to it. Well, then she got off and put the baby down on the block and put the older boy on behind the little one and got on and thought she'd pick up the baby, but the horse would not go to the block with the baby lying on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And she was trying every maneuver she could make with the three children and her husband watched her quite a while and finally he showed himself and he said, wife, what are you trying to do?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: She said, I've stayed here along with the bear and the deer and the panther and the rest of them as long as I can and I'm going somewhere and the horse won't go up to the block. He said, well, let's unsaddle the horse and put it away and I'll be home a while now. So he said...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There's a story that you told me when I was here before that we didn't take down and it was a good one. And that was the story about the wagon stagecoach that was caught in the storm at night and went to the home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How does that story go?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, it was in Montana earlier than the days I was there and I was there when it was quite early, but I saw the house where the people went to and I don't know that it was the same family there or not. But the stage was going from Harlepton to Lewiston, which is quite a distance. And a blizzard overtook the stage and the man said he would go to this house, which he could still see, and see if they could stay overnight. Well, at that time in that country, you didn't ask to stay overnight.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: You went and they said, have you had supper yet?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: We're just going to sit down, sit down with us and we'll fix you a bed. You didn't ask, they offered. But he went in and explained that the blizzard had overtaken him just outside here. And he had a coach of people and could they come in and stay till the blizzard was over. They said no, they didn't take people in. Well, they just weren't going to take him in, that's all there was to it. So he went on thinking maybe he could get to some other place or something. And one of his horses fell dead. And he had a coach of men and a 12 year old girl. And as soon as the horse fell dead, he took all the blankets that were available. All that he had in the coach. And put them down by the horse while it was still warm. And bedded the 12 year old girl there and covered her all up. And then he had the men all circled in a line and all clasped hands. And they circled round that horse with the little girl lying by it. And when it would get to be whatever he thought might be an hour or so, he would tell them that now they could stop for five minutes but not let go of hands and stand right where they were. And he would see about the little girl that she was alright. And she was keeping warm enough with the blankets and had that warmth to start with. And she was asleep. And then he would get into the circle with men and say, we'll circle again for about an hour and then we'll stop for five minutes again. And they kept that up until morning the storm was over.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And they told quite a story about Mrs. Winters. She believed in observing the Sabbath. They were rancher. Well, they were cattle. He was a cattle man out on Beaver Creek.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And he wanted and did always wait until Sunday for his dehorning and branding and so on. Because the other men were available on Sunday. Well, I don't know why they couldn't be available any other day, but they weren't. But anyway, he did all that on Sunday and she tried to talk him out of it that the Sabbath was the Lord's day and it was not for that purpose. But anyway, he'd say, well, I can get the other men here and I need help. So he kept right on with it. So fun. Unless she, one Sunday, with a Bible in her hand, climbed to the top rail of the corral. She didn't say a word to the men at all. She just climbed to the top rail of the corral and she sat there reading aloud. Paying no attention to the men at all, just reading aloud. Well, after a little, one of the men got on his horse and rode away. For soon another man got on his horse and rode away. Well, they quit their work right then. She never said a word about it at all.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: They used to tell that story about it. And for a long time I had the sewing machine. I bought it from Mrs. Winters. The sewing machine that she'd always had. I don't know whether there were any more sewing machines in the country or not. But I had it for years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The story that you just told about the keeping the little girl alive through the night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did that do to the reputation of the family that hadn't taken them in?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
